title: Serpent & Dove vs House of Salt and Sorrows: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Shelby Mahurin's Serpent & Dove vs Erin A. Craig's House of Salt and Sorrows—which fantasy romance is better? Complete comparison.

book1.title: Serpent & Dove
book1.author: Shelby Mahurin
book1.description: Two years ago, Louise le Blanc fled her coven and took shelter in the city of Cesarine, forsaking all magic and living off whatever she could steal. But when Lou is forced into a marriage with Reid Diggory, a witch-hunter who despises everything she is, she finds herself drawn into a world she thought she'd left behind forever.

book2.title: House of Salt and Sorrows
book2.author: Erin A. Craig
book2.description: In a manor by the sea, twelve sisters are cursed. Annaleigh lives a sheltered life at Highmoor, a manor by the sea, with her sisters, their father, and stepmother. Once they were twelve, but loneliness fills the grand halls now that four of the girls' lives have been cut short. Each death was more tragic than the last—the plague, a plummeting fall, a drowning, a slippery plunge—and there are whispers throughout the surrounding villages that the family is cursed by the gods.

book1Strengths: The forced marriage setup is genius: Lou (a witch hiding in Cesarine) gets arrested, Reid (a Chasseur witch-hunter) needs a wife to advance. They marry to solve both problems—she gets protection, he gets promotion—then have to live together while hating everything the other represents. Peak enemies-to-lovers tension

Lou le Blanc is sassy and fun: she's a thief, a liar, uses blood magic (forbidden), and absolutely refuses to be meek. When Reid tries to control her, she steals his clothes. When he lectures about morality, she mocks him. Her irreverence makes the romance entertaining instead of predictable

The witch-hunter world-building is detailed: Chasseurs hunt witches using holy weapons, the Archbishop commands them, Dame Blanche witches are hunted to extinction, and Lou's blood magic is the most forbidden kind. The Church vs witchcraft conflict creates real danger—Reid's brothers would burn Lou alive if they knew

The banter works: Lou calls Reid 'Chass' (short for Chasseur, he hates it), he calls her 'madame wife' sarcastically, they fight about everything from breakfast to bedsharing. The verbal sparring feels authentic to people forced together who genuinely irritate each other

The slow-burn pays off: Reid starts disgusted by Lou (she's everything he's been trained to kill), slowly sees her humanity, then realizes he loves her despite his entire worldview saying she's evil. Watching his religious certainty crumble is satisfying character development
book1Weaknesses: Reid has killed witches—not just arrested them, burned them alive as a Chasseur. Lou forgives this surprisingly quickly given he's murdered people like her. The romance asks you to root for a witch falling for her literal executioner, which some readers can't stomach

At 530 pages, it drags in the middle: the forced marriage tension peaks early, then there's 200 pages of 'will they won't they' before the plot kicks back in. If you need constant action, you'll get impatient during the domestic scenes

Books 2-3 (Blood & Honey, Gods & Monsters) decline significantly: the love triangle addition feels forced, the plot gets convoluted, and the magic system expands until it stops making sense. The trilogy should've been a duology

The magic system lacks clear rules: blood magic can do... whatever the plot needs? Lou's abilities seem inconsistent—sometimes she's powerful, sometimes helpless. You're never sure what's possible, which makes stakes unclear
book2Strengths: The Twelve Dancing Princesses retelling is brilliant: Annaleigh and her 11 sisters once danced at balls every night, but four sisters have died mysteriously (plague, fall, drowning, accident). The surviving sisters still sneak out to magical balls through a door in their room—dancing with ghosts, gods, or something darker. The fairytale framework creates creeping dread

The gothic atmosphere is stunning: Highmoor manor sits on cliffs by the sea, waves crash constantly, salt corrodes everything, the gods Pontus (sea) and Old Maude (death) loom over the family. Craig writes gorgeous prose—the setting feels alive and malevolent, like the house itself is cursed

The mystery works: are the deaths accidents, murders, or divine punishment? Annaleigh investigates while questioning her sanity (is she seeing ghosts or going mad?). The reveals are satisfying—clues planted early pay off, and the culprit's motivation makes twisted sense

The horror elements are real: rotting sisters appear in mirrors, a stepmother might be poisoning them, the youngest sister sees things crawling in the dark, funeral after funeral after funeral. This earns its gothic label—it's genuinely unsettling in ways most YA fantasy isn't

Cassius (the love interest) is a mysterious stranger who appears at the balls. The romance develops slowly because Annaleigh's focused on survival, not kissing. When the romance does happen, it feels earned because they've actually gotten to know each other through the investigation
book2Weaknesses: The pacing is slow for the first half: lots of atmospheric description, sisters dancing, Annaleigh investigating. If you need action immediately, you'll get impatient—the payoff doesn't come until page 250+. Mystery readers love this, romance readers get bored

Some readers guess the twist early: if you're familiar with gothic mysteries, the clues are pretty obvious by the halfway point. The 'who' isn't that shocking, though the 'why' and 'how' still land well. Don't expect Gone Girl-level surprises

The romance is secondary: Cassius appears sporadically, the relationship develops slowly, and there's not much page time for kissing because Annaleigh's busy investigating murders. If you're reading for romance, you'll be disappointed—this is mystery-first

The deaths are disturbing: four sisters dead, detailed descriptions of corpses, funeral scenes, grief and trauma throughout. This isn't light fantasy—it's gothic horror that deals with death constantly. Not great if you're sensitive to that

book1: A kiss for luck, then. And perhaps another after.
Chaos and cunning. That's what we are.
You are my wife. For better or worse, you're mine.
I would burn down the world for you.
Some things are worth the risk.
Magic always comes with a price.
book2: The sea does not like to be restrained.
We are all ghosts of what we once were.
Death is patient. Death is kind. Death will wait.
Some curses cannot be broken. They can only be endured.
Dancing with the dead is never a good idea.

book1: Lou and Reid's banter is entertaining
More romantic focus for romance fans
Perfect for readers wanting lighter fantasy romance
book2: Experience gothic Twelve Dancing Princesses retelling
The atmosphere is haunting and beautiful
Perfect for readers wanting darker fantasy
Shorter and cheaper than Serpent & Dove

verdict.summary: Serpent & Dove wins for romance fans (145,000 ratings at 4.1 stars vs 95,000 at 3.9). The forced marriage enemies-to-lovers (Lou the witch married to Reid the witch-hunter) delivers entertaining banter and slow-burn chemistry. House of Salt and Sorrows wins for atmosphere—the gothic Twelve Dancing Princesses retelling with Highmoor manor, mysterious deaths, and creeping horror is stunning but slower-paced. Different purposes: Serpent & Dove for romance-focused readers, House of Salt and Sorrows for gothic mystery fans.
verdict.recommendation: Read Serpent & Dove if you want enemies-to-lovers with excellent banter. Mahurin created peak forced marriage tension: Lou (a witch using forbidden blood magic) marries Reid (a Chasseur who burns witches alive) for mutual convenience—she needs protection, he needs promotion. They live together hating each other, fight about everything from breakfast to bedsharing, and slowly fall in love despite their entire worldviews being incompatible. Lou's sassy (steals his clothes when he annoys her), Reid's religious certainty crumbles watching her humanity, and the 'Chass' vs 'madame wife' banter works. The 530 pages drag in the middle and Reid's past (he's killed witches) is hard to forgive, but the romance payoff is satisfying. Books 2-3 decline, so treat this as standalone. Read House of Salt and Sorrows if you want gothic fairytale mystery with stunning atmosphere. Craig retells Twelve Dancing Princesses as horror: Annaleigh's four sisters have died mysteriously (plague, drowning, falls), the remaining sisters sneak through a magical door to dance with ghosts or gods, and Highmoor manor on the cliffs feels cursed by Pontus (sea god) and Old Maude (death goddess). The mystery (accident, murder, or divine curse?) unfolds slowly with rotting sisters in mirrors, funeral after funeral, and genuinely unsettling horror. Cassius (mysterious stranger) provides romance but it's secondary to survival. The pacing is slow, some guess the twist early, but the prose is gorgeous. At 406 pages it's quicker than Serpent & Dove. If you only read one, choose Serpent & Dove for romance or House of Salt and Sorrows for atmosphere—they serve different needs entirely.
